citation: Aiello F, Di Claudio C, Fanchini M, Impellizzeri FM, McCall A, Sharp C, Brown SJ. Do non-contact injuries occur during high-speed running in elite football? Preliminary results from a new GPS and video-based method. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport. 2023. 10.1016/j.jsams.2023.07.007
abstract: This retrospective descriptive study evaluated the feasibility of combining GPS and video data to estimate the speed and acceleration at the time of non-contact injuries in elite football players. Data from 46 male elite Serie A players over three seasons were analysed, with training and matches video-recorded and external load measured via Catapult Vector S7 GPS. A total of 34 non-contact injuries were analysed using the Football Injury Inciting Circumstances Classification System (FIICCS), with inciting circumstances coded for speed, acceleration, and activity type.
tldr: This study provides the first GPS-plus-video method to quantify the exact running speed when non-contact injuries occur in elite soccer. The key finding is that nearly all hamstring injuries happened while players ran faster than 25 km/h — above 80% of their individual maximal speed. This means practitioners should not limit high-speed running exposure as an injury prevention strategy; instead, they should systematically prepare players to tolerate and decelerate from maximal sprint velocities. The method itself is a practical template for any club with GPS and video infrastructure to conduct standardised injury circumstance analysis.
methods: Retrospective descriptive study. 46 male elite players (mean age 26.6 ± 4.3 years, height 185 ± 6.5 cm, weight 80.8 ± 6.3 kg) from one Serie A football club over three seasons. External load collected via Catapult Vector S7 GPS (10 Hz). Training and matches video-recorded via fixed AXIS P1448-LE camera system and TV broadcasts. Injuries diagnosed by team physician. Inciting circumstances classified using FIICCS. Speed and average acceleration computed manually from GPS data for short-duration activities. Descriptive statistics reported as median and IQR.
findings: 34 non-contact injuries were analysed (34 of 68 total had complete data). Of 17 hamstring injuries, 16 occurred during running with a median peak speed of 29.28 km/h (IQR: 26.61–31.13 km/h), corresponding to 87.55% of maximal speed (IQR: 78.5–89.75%). All 17 hamstring running injuries occurred at peak speeds >25 km/h. Players were accelerating <2 m/s² in 8 cases and >2 m/s² in 3 cases (median acceleration 1.74 m/s²); decelerating >2 m/s² in 4 cases and <2 m/s² in 1 case. Both quadriceps injuries occurred during kicking. Adductor injuries occurred during acceleration, deceleration, and kicking. Most injuries (82%) occurred during matches; 97% occurred in players who had played from the start of the session.
limitations: Retrospective design prevented systematic player interviews immediately post-injury, requiring assumption that injuries occurred at peak speed. Acceleration data for short activity periods had to be calculated manually (Δv/Δt) as the software could not extract it automatically. 37% (30/80) of originally reported non-contact injuries could not be analysed due to missing GPS or video data. Reliability of the FIICCS classification system was not formally evaluated. Data from a single club only, limiting generalisability.
practicalImplications: Limiting players' high-speed running exposure is unlikely to reduce hamstring injury risk since most injuries occur at speeds >25 km/h and above 80% of individual maximal speed. NFL and college football staff should ensure players regularly experience and are conditioned for maximal sprint speeds and high-speed deceleration in training. Combining GPS and video to classify injury inciting circumstances is feasible and provides actionable data for designing injury prevention and rehabilitation protocols. Training programmes should prepare players for decelerating from sprint speeds, not just accelerating to them.
athleteDev: Football players at all development levels should be progressively exposed to high-speed running (>25 km/h) and sprint-to-deceleration sequences in training. The finding that 97% of injuries occurred in players who started the session suggests fatigue management and late-game conditioning are important athlete development targets. Periodised speed development that includes maximal-velocity work and high-intensity deceleration loading is indicated.
rtp: Return-to-play progressions after hamstring, adductor, or quadriceps injury should include running exposures that reach and exceed 80% of the athlete's individual maximal speed before full clearance, as this is the zone in which most non-contact injuries occur. Video-GPS analysis of the original injury inciting circumstance can guide task-specific RTP criteria. Deceleration capacity from sprint speed should be specifically tested before return to full training.

citation: Pimenta R, Antunes H, Lopes T, Veloso A. Do Repeated Sprints Affect the Biceps Femoris Long Head Architecture in Football Players with and without an Injury History? — A Retrospective Study. Biology. 2023;12:96. 10.3390/biology12010096
abstract: NOTE: This file has a truncated filename ('retrospective st.pdf') but contains identical content to ID 151. This retrospective study compared biceps femoris long head (BFlh) muscle architecture — fascicle length (FL), pennation angle (PA), and muscle thickness (MT) — between 12 professional football players with a history of BFlh strain injury and 20 healthy controls, before and after a 10 × 30 m repeated sprint protocol. Ultrasound B-mode imaging was used to assess architecture at rest and during active contraction (20% MVIC) pre- and post-sprint task, and knee flexor peak torque was measured via dynamometry.
tldr: This study demonstrates that previously injured football players maintain shorter BFlh fascicle lengths and greater pennation angles compared to healthy controls and their own contralateral limbs, even after returning to full training and competition. Intriguingly, the previously injured players were actually faster during repeated sprints. These architectural differences — likely a consequence of the original injury rather than a pre-existing risk factor — mean the injured limb may generate less force at the long muscle lengths required during sprinting, potentially increasing re-injury risk. Repeated sprint exercise itself had limited impact on BFlh architecture in both groups. For football staff, this reinforces the importance of long-term monitoring of BFlh fascicle length after hamstring injury, even in fully 'cleared' players.
methods: Retrospective study; 32 male professional football players (age 25.03 ± 3.72 years, height 1.79 ± 0.07 m, mass 74.69 ± 8.67 kg). Injury group: 12 players with unilateral BFlh strain history within last 11.7 ± 7.4 months (range 2–23 months). Control group: 20 players with no BFlh history. All returned to pre-injury training and competition levels. BFlh architecture (FL, PA, MT) assessed via two Aixplorer v11 ultrasound scanners at ~50% femur length. Protocol: 30-min rest, warm-up, 2 resting videos per limb, active videos at 20% MVIC, then 10 × 30 m repeated sprint task (30 s rest between sprints), followed by repeat measurements and 2 MVIC. Dynamometry measured knee flexor peak torque (1000 Hz). Data analysed with IBM SPSS 27.0; two-way mixed repeated measures ANOVA.
findings: Injured players had significantly shorter FL at rest (p = 0.014; η²p = 0.196) and in active state (p < 0.001; η²p = 0.413), and greater PA at rest (p = 0.002; η²p = 0.307) and active state (p < 0.001; η²p = 0.368) vs. controls. Intra-individual: injured limbs had shorter FL at rest (p = 0.012; η²p = 0.519) and active state (p = 0.039; η²p = 0.332) and greater PA in passive (p < 0.001; η²p = 0.732) and active states (p = 0.018; η²p = 0.412) vs. contralateral limbs. Injured players were significantly faster during sprints (7.00 ± 0.25 vs. 6.82 ± 0.19 m/s; p = 0.026). No significant differences in peak torque between groups. Sprint task had limited impact on BFlh architectural parameters; contralateral FL decreased and PA increased post-sprint in resting conditions for both groups.
limitations: Results apply only to senior professional male football players. The static ultrasound technique may overestimate FL by ~0.5 cm (especially for FL >7 cm) due to the transducer width and linear extrapolation method. Cannot determine whether shorter BFlh FL is a consequence of injury or a pre-existing risk factor due to retrospective design. Muscle and tendon stiffness were not measured. Small sample sizes.
practicalImplications: Football staff should not assume BFlh architecture has normalised after a player returns to full training following hamstring injury. Shorter fascicle lengths in the previously injured limb persist and may reduce force-generating capacity at the long muscle lengths encountered during sprinting. Regular ultrasound monitoring of BFlh FL after hamstring injury — including comparison to the contralateral limb — is recommended. The absence of between-group differences in peak torque suggests isometric strength testing alone is insufficient to detect residual structural deficits post-injury.
athleteDev: Developmental football players who sustain hamstring strain injuries may carry residual BFlh architectural changes that predispose to re-injury. Programmes targeting fascicle length development — such as long-length eccentric training (e.g., Nordic curls, Romanian deadlifts at full range) — should be incorporated into post-injury development plans even after medical clearance. Monitoring BFlh FL via ultrasound longitudinally may identify players at elevated re-injury risk.
rtp: Return-to-play decision-making after BFlh strain should include assessment of BFlh fascicle length via ultrasound, not just strength or functional performance testing. The finding that injured players had shorter FL and greater PA than their contralateral limb even months after RTP suggests structural normalisation is not guaranteed by standard RTP protocols. Consider bilateral ultrasound assessment as a supplementary RTP criterion, with the goal of reducing the FL asymmetry between limbs before clearing for full competition exposure.

citation: Crow J, Semciw A, Couch J, Pizzari T. Does a recent hamstring muscle injury affect the timing of muscle activation during high speed overground running in professional Australian football players? Physical Therapy in Sport. 2020;43:188–194. 10.1016/j.ptsp.2020.03.005
abstract: This cohort study investigated whether the temporal characteristics of hamstring and gluteal muscle activation are altered during high-speed overground running in professional Australian Football players following hamstring muscle injury. Seven players with a recent hamstring injury (HS group; injury within past 6 months) and eight uninjured controls (CON group) from the same AFL club performed high-speed runs at approximately 90% of perceived maximum speed on a 100 m grass track. Surface EMG recorded muscle onset timing, offset timing, and duration of activation for the biceps femoris, medial hamstrings, and gluteus maximus.
tldr: Professional AFL players who completed comprehensive rehabilitation after a hamstring injury showed no differences in the timing of hamstring or gluteal muscle activation during high-speed running compared to uninjured teammates. This reassuring finding suggests that when rehabilitation is thorough — including high-speed running progressions — the neuromuscular sequencing of hamstring activation during sprinting is likely restored. For football staff, this underscores that the persistent altered muscle timing patterns reported in recreational athletes following hamstring injury may be a rehabilitation quality issue rather than an inevitable consequence of injury, supporting investment in high-quality, sport-specific RTP protocols.
methods: Cohort study; field-based testing. 15 male professional AFL players from one club: 7 with hamstring injury in the preceding 6 months (HS group; mean 108 ± 46 days since strain), 8 uninjured controls (CON group). All injuries MRI-confirmed. Participants ran 100 m at 60%, 70%, 80%, 90% of perceived maximum speed with 100 m walk recovery, then completed one practice trial. Two testing sessions within 7 days. Surface EMG via Delsys Trigno Wireless system (CMRR >80 dB; 2000 Hz; band-pass 20–450 Hz) on gluteus maximus, biceps femoris, medial hamstrings. Footswitches on plantar hallux and inter-phalangeal joint. GPS (MinimaxX Team 2.5, Catapult) for running speed. Two middle-20 m strides per trial selected for analysis (10 strides × 5 trials per participant). Muscle onset/offset at 15% peak amplitude threshold; duration as percentage of gait cycle. Mann–Whitney U tests and effect sizes (z-score/√n).
findings: No statistically significant differences between HS and CON groups for muscle onset, offset, or duration of activation for gluteus maximus, biceps femoris, or medial hamstrings (all p > 0.05). Groups were matched for age (HS: 22.3 ± 1.6; CON: 21.9 ± 1.6 years), height, weight, and average running speed (HS: 7.3 ± 0.3 m/s; CON: 7.2 ± 0.4 m/s). The grand ensemble EMG curves for all three muscles showed nearly identical patterns between groups throughout the gait cycle. These null results were consistent across both testing sessions.
limitations: Small sample from a single club limits generalisability. Could not control for training volume in the week preceding testing. EMG sensor dislodgement from one participant's medial hamstrings (CON group) reduced that muscle's sample to n=7 in CON. Study used onset/offset amplitude threshold approach rather than normalised amplitude, so differences in activation level rather than timing could have been missed. Overground GPS-confirmed running speeds (~7.3 m/s) may not represent true maximal sprint velocity. Environmental conditions (temperature, wind, ground hardness) were not fully controlled.
practicalImplications: After comprehensive hamstring rehabilitation, professional football players appear to restore normal neuromuscular timing during high-speed running. This provides confidence that well-rehabilitated players are not carrying covert neuromuscular deficits at the point of RTP. Football staff should use this as validation for investing in thorough, progressive rehabilitation that includes sport-speed running rather than settling for pain-free jogging. Residual activation timing deficits identified in recreational athletes may reflect inadequate rehabilitation rather than unavoidable injury sequelae.
athleteDev: Young football players recovering from hamstring strain should progress through a full high-speed running rehabilitation continuum. The data suggest that those who complete comprehensive rehabilitation programmes restore normal muscle activation patterns, indicating the nervous system is highly adaptable with appropriate loading. Coaches should not restrict athletes from high-speed running in late-stage rehabilitation out of fear of altered motor patterns, as this may ironically delay neuromuscular recovery.
rtp: This study supports returning players to full high-speed running after comprehensive hamstring rehabilitation without persistent concern about altered muscle timing patterns. RTP criteria should include demonstrated ability to run at high speeds (>7 m/s) without restriction, with confidence that normal gluteal and hamstring activation sequencing is likely restored. Practical implication for NFL/college football: do not rely on neuromuscular timing as an RTP screen unless the athlete is known to have had incomplete rehabilitation; instead focus on strength, range of motion, and sport-specific running load tolerance.

citation: Guitart-Trench M, Sanchez-Sanchez J, Valle X, Garcia-Unanue J, Cos F, Alonso-Callejo A, Rodas G, Felipe JL. Does accumulated physical load in different time windows affect hamstring injuries in elite football players? Research in Sports Medicine. 2025. 10.1080/15438627.2025.2468799
abstract: This descriptive longitudinal study investigated how accumulated training load over 7-, 14-, and 28-day time windows influences the incidence of hamstring injuries in 343 elite academy football players (2nd team, U-19, U-18) from a Spanish La Liga club competing in UEFA Champions League across four seasons (2017/18–2020/21). External load variables — total distance (TD), high-speed running (HSR), accelerations, decelerations, sprint count, and high metabolic load (HML) — were collected via GPS (WIMU PRO) and compared between injury blocks and pre-injury blocks for each time window. The acute:chronic workload ratio (ACWR) was also analysed.
tldr: Only the 28-day accumulated load window reliably discriminated between injured and non-injured periods in elite youth/development football players — the 7- and 14-day windows showed no significant differences. Players sustained hamstring injuries after 28-day periods of significantly higher total distance, HSR, sprint counts, acceleration/deceleration load, and high metabolic load compared to the preceding 28-day block. The ACWR was not associated with injury severity. For football staff this means that short-term workload spikes captured in weekly monitoring may be insufficient — chronic 28-day load accumulation, particularly in high-speed and sprint volume, should be the primary monitoring target for hamstring injury risk management.
methods: Descriptive longitudinal study; 343 male academy players from three teams of one Spanish La Liga club (2nd Team, U-19, U-18) across four seasons (2017/18 to 2020/21). Total 27,895 h training sessions, 1,488 h friendly matches, 6,551 h official matches. GPS data collected via WIMU PRO (RealtrackSystems, Spain; ICC x: 0.65, y: 0.85). External load variables: TD, MIR (18–21 km/h), HIR (21–24 km/h), HSR (>21 km/h), sprint distance (>24 km/h), sprint count, HI-ACC (>3 m/s²), HI-DCC (<−3 m/s²), HML (>25 W/kg²). Time windows: 7, 14, 28 days; injury block vs. pre-injury block. Injuries per UEFA consensus guidelines; classified using OSICS_10. Linear mixed models for repeated measurement; independent t-test for group comparisons; ACWR rolling 7:28. IBM SPSS 28.0.
findings: The 28-day time window showed significant differences between injury and pre-injury blocks for all analysed variables (p < 0.05): TD (+13,605.61 m; ES: 0.50), HSR (+725.41 m; ES: 0.48), HI-ACC (+119.42 m; ES: 0.49), HI-DCC (+133.99 m; ES: 0.48), sprint count (+18.81; ES: 0.48), HML (+2,689.54 m; ES: 0.55). Speed zone distances at 18–21 km/h (+581.83 m; ES: 0.51), 21–24 km/h (+333.08 m; ES: 0.52), and >24 km/h (+204.40 m; ES: 0.37) were also significantly higher. The 7- and 14-day windows showed no significant differences (p > 0.05). Hamstring muscle injury players showed higher load than medical attention players across all time windows for HSR and sprint variables. ACWR was not significantly different between injury and medical attention groups.
limitations: Study confined to academy players (2nd team and youth squads) from a single club, limiting generalisability to senior professionals. Goalkeepers were excluded. Individual variability in load response was not fully addressed despite linear mixed model use. Injuries classified as grade 0 (not forcing absence) were included alongside time-loss injuries, which may dilute findings. ACWR methodology has been criticised for its statistical properties in the broader literature.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches in football should extend their workload monitoring window to 28 days for hamstring injury risk assessment rather than relying exclusively on acute weekly loads. When the 28-day accumulated load — particularly HSR distance, sprint count, and HML — spikes significantly above the preceding 28-day period, hamstring injury risk is elevated. Weekly fluctuations (7-day windows) are insufficient predictors. Staff should compare current 28-day blocks to the prior 28-day block for each player, not to a seasonal average, when making rotation or load management decisions.
athleteDev: Development players (U-18, U-19, reserve teams) accumulating high chronic loads over 28-day blocks — especially in sprint and high-speed running volume — face elevated hamstring injury risk. Programming for these age groups should build sprint and high-speed running capacity progressively across the season, avoiding abrupt 28-day load spikes. Periodisation models should account for match congestion periods, which can compress recovery and cause undetected chronic load accumulation in shorter monitoring windows.
rtp: When returning a player from hamstring injury to full training and match play, the accumulated load of the 28-day re-introduction period should be carefully managed to avoid surpassing the chronic load baseline from the prior 28-day period. The absence of ACWR differences between injury groups suggests ratio-based monitoring alone is insufficient for RTP load management decisions; absolute 28-day volume targets — particularly for HSR and sprint distance — should be established and tracked for each athlete returning from hamstring injury.
